institution: Sector-wide typology - Indian retail digital payments
source_documents: https://www.rbi.org.in (Master Direction - Digital Payment Security Controls, Feb 2021)
category: Account takeover via phishing, credential stuffing, or malware
risk_signals: Login from a new device or unusual geolocation, often followed by changes to registered email/phone
Registered contact details or beneficiaries changed shortly before an outbound transfer
Transaction alerts disabled or redirected around the time of the transfer
analyst_reasoning: Once an attacker holds valid credentials, the standard playbook is to change contact details (to intercept alerts), add a new beneficiary, and transfer during or just after the cooling period. The signature is the sequence: new-device login, profile change, beneficiary add, transfer.
investigation_steps: Correlate device fingerprint, IP, and geolocation of the suspect session against the customer's baseline
Reconstruct the profile-change -> beneficiary-add -> transfer sequence and its timing
Check whether new-payee cooling-off controls were bypassed or waived
decision: Force step-up authentication, freeze outbound transfers, reverse contact-detail changes, and re-verify the customer out-of-band.
outcome: Funds move to mule beneficiaries; the faster the profile-change anomaly is caught, the more is recoverable.
lessons_learned: A profile change immediately followed by a beneficiary add and transfer is a high-confidence takeover pattern
Cooling-off periods on new beneficiaries are a key control and must not be silently waived
Alert-suppression around a transfer is itself a red flag
